quotes.quote: Nothing has worked and now I'm just frustrated. I go to the range and tinker all day long and nothing works consistently. I still sent 70%+ of my drives with either a fade or it starts right and slices hard right.
I just can't square the clubface no matter if I have a stiff shaft/heavier club head and a rolled over grip.

I took a lesson, he corrected my grip and turned my left hand over the grip, my thumbs are no longer straight down the grip. He even told me to close my front shoulder, so now I feel like I'm curling into a ball at the address. Still, slice/push everything.
For everyone saying the G400 Max is a 'fairway finder'....not for me. I find nothing easier/better about this driver than I did my 2011 TaylorMade Rocketballz driver.

I already got with a instructor, he changed my grip and closed my front shoulder. He also told me to back up and reach for the ball at address with my arms all the way straight. Still nothing consistent even after multiple range trips and rounds. That's why I'm confused. Felt like I was fed a bunch of nonsense.

I've tried everything in my power to shallow the club out. Almost rotating it around my body in the backswing. I still couldn't get anything to be straight. If anything that caused more problems with topping the ball and having no control of striking the ball in the middle of the clubface when it comes back around in your downswing.

That's the weird part, I feel like I'm slowly taking the club head straight back. I even follow the club head with my eyes for a split second on my takeaway to almost guide it straight back. Then I see a video of myself and it's like holy s*** that is over the top.
I just physically don't know how to get a more neutral swing path because my muscle memory takes over and/or I can't see myself swinging so I don't know if I'm doing it correctly.
Nothing fixed your slice because your swing is a huge slice machine. Just because you feel like you're doing the advice from the instructor doesn't mean you're actually doing it.
In my swing, I need to mentally give myself really stupid feels to actually do what I need to do and most times they are the OPPOSITE of what my body physically does.
Right now I have too much baseball in my swing. I do a lot of controlling of the club with my dominant right arm--which is where the chicken wing comes from.
You cannot build a good golf swing by assembling a random assortment of tips and fixes from different users. The net result could be a Franken-swing where the parts and pieces don't fit.
I know my golf swing ADHD has taken over at the range, and after 100 balls I accomplished absolutely nothing.
I dont slice my irons, but slice the hell out of my driver...what am I doing wrong? I've tried everything short of dropping down some money for a lesson (which I'm not entirely keen on).

I've tried moving the ball way up in my stance (as in, even with my left foot if not in front), as well as taking my right foot and moving it backwards a tad. Nothing works. Should I look into a shorter driver?

I'm still struggling with the same issue - one thing I realized in my quest for the straight drive was that only one thing causes a slice: an open clubface at impact... unfortunately, there are probably no less than 50 things that can cause you to open the clubface prior to impact.

I sometimes get the 'ultra fade' on the driver as well late in the round despite having a nice small consistent fade on all irons. Usually when I don't complete backswing later in round and/or legs/hips get tired and stop firing through.
This thread makes me laugh at the recognition that there are so many things that can go wrong with a golf swing.

I've found that on my worst shots, I'm winding up too much by bending my left elbow at the top to compensate for not being able to turn my shoulders as much. As a result, my club dips below parallel and when I start down, my hands are too far behind the ball.
You've tried every instructor, every tip, every drill to fix your hook, but nothing works. Your swing path looks good, your clubface appears square, yet you still snap hook shots off the planet.

When amateurs come to me and tell me they've tried everything to gain distance and NOTHING works... Things like increasing the length of your swing... Or producing more rotation in the backswing... Or learning how to hit up on the ball...
No more hitting a 3 wood off the tee... No more swinging low and slow to maintain control... No more embarrassment about your driving distance...
You swing over-the-top... You lose clubface control... You miss the sweet spot... You ruin your tempo.
You try to swing 'slow and smooth' - so you can keep your swing intact... But then slower swing speeds OBVIOUSLY means less distance.

I'm sure you - like all my other students - have TRIED swinging harder... And maybe it works once or twice to get the extra distance you want... But then as you try swinging harder hole after hole... That's where the wheels come off.

You're releasing ALL your swing speed BEFORE you get to the ball... Which is what happens when you swing on an outside-in path and flip your wrists BEFORE impact... And the result is a dead pull left.
You're releasing ALL your swing speed into the GROUND... Which is what happens when you - like 95% of all amateurs - are swinging on a downward angle of attack - with a driver.
This results in an open club face... leading to the dreaded slice... pushed short drive... or a pop-up that barely gets past the ladies' tee.
The swing is out of control within the first second and has to correct itself all through the delivery... you come across the ball creating wicked side spin resulting in your slice.

When I had a time where I did hit it straight-- I 'snapped' my wrists in my downswing to close the face over right at impact.. Problem with that is you have to snap your wrists at the perfect time. Doing that gets some laser straight shots, but is still far from consistent.
There's no equipment that will cure a slice. You adjust your equipment to fine tune things, not resolve swing issues. Work on the swing with an instructor, it's generally not an overnight process.
It can take a lot of time and effort to make the change. It's not something that's going to happen over the course of a few swings or even several buckets of balls. It might take months or longer.
Feel isn't always real - What you feel changing isn't always a real or accurate representation of what really is happening.
You cannot fix everything at once. It might take 3-5 range sessions to ingrain a single change in your golf swing.
Equipment can mitigate tendencies, but is not a corrective measure. I'd recommend building the swing you want to have first, then get fitted second.

I have a faster swing speed than average but I'm not swinging out of my shoes. Not sure what it is but I would guess around 110. I replaced the lighter Alta shaft with the Hazardous Black stiff shaft... I even bought a heavier weight for the G400 club head thinking a combination of stiff shaft + heavy weight head + lower loft would get me to hit less slices.
I bought all of it thinking it would help and get away from the lighter 'whippy' downswing that sends balls spinning off the face of the earth.
Back swing is too straight up and not at that 45 degree angle and when I come down--i come down outside to in.

I had issue with properly shifting my weight to the back leg on the backswing. A lot of my weight was still on front leg which caused a steeper backswing/downswing. Since you can somewhat get away with a steep downswing with your irons, on the driver, it'll cause big slices.
If you believe you have a good swing, my guess is you have a regular shaft. Thus the club head is just open on impact and simply not catching up to the swing speed.

I think a shaft that is too flexy will also yield the occasional, unwieldy hook. I had a huge problem with my reg flex driver that I had received as a gift and was playing off the rack. I went and got my swing speed measured and was topping out at 120!

Hard to guess at what your problem is since there are obviously so many possibilities, but when I get into a funk I'm the same way. The most recent and effective fix I've made to correct this was to concentrate on my left wrist on the backswing.
Irons are a hell of a lot more difficult to slice than a driver because of the loft(s). You're not going to like this, but I would suggest hitting a 3W until you're extremely confident with it.
You are clearing your hips too early... and that could be because you're taking a longer backswing, and so your arms can't catch back up.

The unfixable hook mystery: Tried multiple instructors without success. Swing mechanics appear correct on video. Path and plane look good. Clubface seems square at address. Still hitting massive hooks.
Hesitation or hitch at top of backswing. Multiple practice swings look perfect. Only hooks with ball present. Timing issues with transition. Charles Barkley-style pause at top.
Why traditional fixes fail: Focus on swing mechanics. Overlook grip and hand action. Assume setup is maintained throughout. Don't observe subtle hand movements. Treat symptoms rather than cause.
The Biggest Problem In Amateur Golf... Is A Lack Of Distance. And I'm not just talking about the embarrassment it causes... Or how it ruins your confidence...
Guys with the longest drives... have the lowest handicaps, BY FAR.

The club is NOT dropping into the slot... That's so important I'm going to say that again... The ONLY reason you or anyone else on the planet can try to swing harder and yet NEVER gain distance... Is because your club is NOT dropping in the slot.

There's definitely no need for you to continue swinging steeply... And no need to search endlessly through contradictory shallowing tips on YouTube or magazines... Tips that force you into an unnatural swing change that takes forever to ingrain.
I'm now 70 yrs of age and am wowing others in my group who are now increasingly struggling with age related limitations.
Your game - and your driving distance - will be in exactly the same place 6 months from now...
No more spinning your wheels... No more making the same mistakes... And no more getting the same scores you've been getting... round after round.
You're no longer scared to step up to the tee with your driver...
